preamble: The phonetic transformation from the diphthong /ai/ to the open-mid front unrounded vowel /ɛ/ is a common historical sound change, primarily characterized by monophthongization. This process involves the simplification of a two-part vowel sound into a single, unified vowel. The shift often results from the reduction or absorption of the second element of the diphthong, leading to a new monophthong whose quality is influenced by the original components.
phoneticEffects: Monophthongization, diphthong reduction, vowel fronting, vowel lowering (from the /i/ component's influence) or raising (from the /a/ component's influence towards a mid-vowel position)

examples.note: The Ancient Greek diphthong /ai/ monophthongized to /eː/ in Koine Greek. Inscriptional evidence from Attic and Ionic variants confirms the gradual nature of these mergers, where the second element of the diphthong (/i/) centralized and fused with the first, leading to /ɛ/ and later /e/.

In Classical Latin, the diphthong ⟨ae⟩ (originally pronounced [ai̯] or [aɪ̯]) was reduced to the simple vowel [ɛ] during the Roman Empire. Its reflexes in Romance languages point to a monophthong *ɛ in Proto-Romance.
Modern French underwent monophthongization, and the digraph ⟨ai⟩, which formerly represented a diphthong, now represents the sound /ɛ/ or /e/. For example, 'lait' (milk) is pronounced /lɛ/.

The vowel represented graphically as ऐ (romanized as 'ai') in Hindustani has been variously transcribed as [ɛː] or [æː]. Some Eastern dialects retain diphthongal pronunciations [aɪ~əɪ], implying monophthongization to [ɛː] in other dialects.
